CURRENT AFFAIRS:

We may assume that every reader of thie Christmas
Number of the LEADER sees several other Christmas
numbers of home or foreign manufacture. So we may
safely calculate that they are pretty well sick of and
bored with the old crock stories, by the old crock
writers, where the snow falls, and other most unusual,
but most conventional, things occur. We give them a
breezy and original Christmas Number. It is a good-
humouted number, with here and there just a bit of
a sting and some wise reflections for the country’s
good. It is a thoroughly Trish number in so far as the

“ present rather Anglicised: state of this' country will
permit of anything being' Irish; it is thoroughly Trish
in so far as it is so un-English and so concerned’ with
Treland, that' we do not believe the ordinary English-
man could understand many items in it. ~ We have
done our best to give our readers a bright, light and
seasonable publication, and; as we-feel certain that they
will enjoy it, we are content; and’ wish.them all a
« pleasant’ time with it.

There once was a peeler of tact,
Who often' caught Bung in' the- act,
And so you may guess;

the industrial, national and moral welfare of Ireland,

cate. The D.I. said he objected to the renewal of the
certificate on the grounds that the club was not con-

premises; that the club had been conducted in a dis-
orderly manner, and that persons who were not mem-
bers were frequently admitted to the club premises

Linm 8. Gogan writes:—On Friday, 13th inst., the
wate of the National University will meet to con-
sider (amongst other things) a resolution of Convocation
urging the re-establishment of evening lectures. The
resolution, it will be remembered, was moved by Dr.
McWalter and passed by a majority of 39. The issue
of the meeting is awaited with more than ordinary in-
terest by the large number of students who are unable
to secure the advantages of a University education on
account of the demands made upon them by business
or professional duties and the present obligation requir-
ing attendance at lectures during the day. That atten-
dance at lectures is not only desirable, but necessary
if the Univer is to fulfill its function, few will
doubt, still the injustice of closing its doors to those
who have only the evening at their disposal is self-
evident and must be removed. Now, instead of devot-
ing his spare time in the pursuit of knowledge, the
average young man spends it in the pursuit of amuse-
ment : much laudable ambition is thus crushed for lack
of field for developing it, and an irreparable loss is done
to the individual, and the harm done to the country is
correspondingly great. The matter has already been
taken up by a committee which has undertalen to
organise public opinion. It has already circularised the
members of the Senate with a view to getting them to
report favourably to the Governing Body, with whom
the final settling of the question remains. We have
no doubt as to the result. When the National Univer-
sity came into existence it was hailed as the Poor Man’s
University by those who were not poor. Thanks to
the generosity and patriotism of our Councils, the title
has been in part justified, but. only in part. No con-
sideration whatever is taken of those who are obliged
towvork for their livelihood, comprising tradesmen, com-
mercial men, teachers, clerks, ete., a largs and important
body of citizens. That this demand has the support of a
large number, if indeed not all (and why not all?), the
students in University College is quite evident from
the address of the Auditer of the Literary and Histori-
cal Society. We would urge the Auditor and the mem-
bers of his society to take more definite action in this
matter to secure for their colleagues outside the great
advantages they themselves enjoy. In passing, it is
suggested that this would furnish a good notif for the
('.\'(':II'IIIS]II.II(‘H‘. of a general Students” Council; student

Graduates, iis totally unrepresented on the Governing

REMEMBER INDUSTRY.

FPVHIS number is no place for too much serious

matter.  But it is just the proper place to
remind our readers to support Irish industry during the
Christmas shopping season. None of our readers should
buy his or her Christmas presents before having made
an extra careful study of the advertisements in the
Leaper.  And we might say that, as a Christmas pre-
sent of Irish manufacture, uniting extreme quality and
extreme cheapness, it would be hard, if not impossible,
to beat the Christmas Number of the LEADER.

It cannot be too often insisted upon that Ireland
must develop her industrial side if she is to live and
grow as a healthy organism. The work before the
native Irish is to develop into as complete a national
organism as circumstances permit. A one-sided
arrangement by which the native Irish will raise raw
material and buy finished articles, or in so far as it
does not import the latter, allow their manufacture to
be carried on and exploited by strangers within the
gates, spells the beginning of the end of even such an
attenuated nation as we possess to-day.  Without
Home Rule or with Home Rule the industrial world is
to the enterprising and energetic. The Boer War, so
far as we understand, was brought about by the fact
that the Boers stuck mainly to the land, whilst the
British exploited the mines. There was a consequent
clash.  With Home Rule or without it the future of
Ireland rests with the men who work Ireland, and if
the native race allow strangers to develop the commer-
cial and industrial sides, the influence of the strangers
will be uppermost. Making oneself a free present to
America, and sending back a few dollars to this Fund
or that Fund, is not nation-making. Ireland is for the
men who stay in it, and come into it and make their
living and homes in it. If it is left to the non-Irish to
develop Treland, then Ireland is lost to the native Irish.
Mr. William Martin Murphy, of the ‘‘ Independent,””
and chairman of the Dublin Chamber of Commerce,
speaking at a Scotch dinner in Dublin, in honour of
St. Andrew’s Day, said:—“If one were to take the
Scottish element out of the trade and commerce of
Dublin there would not be a great deal left.”” If that
be true, and there is a certain amount of truth in it at
least, it indicates the leeway that the native Irish have
to make up. When one Scotchman settles here he is
likely to get a place for another one, and if we don’t
want to be edged out of trade and commerce and in-
dustry, we must bestir ourselves. What with the rush
for petty Government jobs, an overstock of lawyers,
doctors, who are doctors because they think it ““‘tony **;
‘“ walking gentlemen’’ of various degrees, the indus-
trial and commercial prospects of the native Irish give
cause for serious consideration. If we do not develop
our industry and commerce we cannot perpetuate the
Trish nation by selling store cattle to English and
Scotch fatteners. If we do not work up our own in-
dustry and commerce the days of the Irish Nation,
Home Rule or no Home Rule, are numbered. That is
something our readers might turn over in their minds
at odd serious moments during the Christmas festivities.

The pleasant gathering then broke up, vowing to re-
assemble and enjoy such another evening as soon as
possible. Loud satisfaction was - expressed with
regard to the manner in which the entertainment
had passed off, and the speech of Mr. Constant Reader
was praised on all sides. As one of the company tersely
put it: * We could do with a good many more Constant
Readers.”’

And so say all of us. As the company were about to
disperse a voice in the adjoining room was heard sing-
ing the following :—

Green Press of my country, in darkness I find thee;
Cant, humbug and cowardice hang o’er thee long.
The thoughtful of Ireland in truth never mind thee,
But leave thee alone to thine ignorant throng.
When Lrin at last to her goal is proceeding,
And straight is the march of each brave-hearted Gael,
The Green Press of Ireland that ought to be leading
But hangs like a mill-stone behind at the tail.

Green Press of my country, how hushed were thy pages
When Norway the Dump was shoved on to our shore,
Though oft hast thou gone into clamours and rages
O’er things on which Irishmen set little store.
While others were fighting a bigoted faction
For fair play and tolerance in their own land,
The aid that thou gavest was flabbiest action,
And what was achieved owes no thanks to thy hand.

Green Press of my country, thy praise may be sounded

By Bungs, and by betting men, bruisers and jocks.
By provers of tolerance, spouters unbounded,

And all who have kept our poor isle on the rocks.
But Treland’s true workers together long banding

Her wrongs the more subtle and deep to redress,
The fame of her papers too well understanding

Will smile with contempt at the name of her Press.

THE LeaDper to hear a few of my impressions of this
famous spot. Vendée is the land of Royalists. From the
daysof Louis X VI., all through the Revolution, the Ven-
déens shed their blood unstintingly for the cause. Not
from any warlike spirit, for in truth they had the greatest
repugnance to military service and to everything that
took them from the farm and the foyer. All through
Vendée it was loyalty pure and simple, that took them
out to fight the Bleus, and every time, as soon as a battle
was lost or won, they hurried back to their homes. Vendée
is divided into three parts—namely, Le Bocage, La Plaine,
and Le Marais. The first, as its name suggests, is prac-
tically covered with trees. From my window I look out
oo what seems to be an enormous park, stretching even to
the horizon.

of coal is not felt. Already beside every cottage the pile
of winter wood is growing. The inhabitants of the

the little village I know best, numbering 1,000 souls,
the presence of even one gendarme is not necessary. Ilach
Wwhite cottage covered with clustering vines, on which now
the fruit hangs thick, seems an abode of peace. When
I first heard this wonderful fact I ventured to ask, ‘“ but
what happens if par hasard a man drinks too much ? ”
“Oh ! well, his neighbour takes care of him,” was the
reply. One almost hears the words *“ go thou and do
likewise.”

Outside many of the cottages, as well as, the vine, one
finds strong apples and pears growing against the wall.
I marvelled at first, travelling through different villages,
why, from time to time, I saw here and there, one cottage
with a dry withered bush hanging over the doorway. I
learned on inquiry that the bush signified to the thirsty
traveller that within he could find wherewith to refresh
himself. In the village M. le Curé dwells in a pretty
presbyteére beside the church. e seems to be on the most
{riendly terms with his flock.

““ Knowing every honest face of theirs
As well as ever shepherd knew his sheep,
And every homely secret in their hearts.”

The question of ¢ the stipend ”’ causes no excitement
here. The bishop sends it direct to the Curé, while the
latter has four collections (or queles), the result of which he
in turn remits to the Bishop. In Vendée there seems to
be a violent dislike to the école laique. In the villages
and towns there is always found the école libre, supported
by the wealthy Catholics in opposition. My little village
has three schools, one laique for boys with a Protestant
teacher, to which alas ! all the male youth of the village
20, as there is no other for them. The two remaining
schools are for girls, the école laique boasting of two pupils
and the école libre over sixty !

Motoring  through Vendée from the Bocage to the
Marais one is struck by many evidences of Catholicity,
especially by the large Crucifix erected outside each village.
The Marais is that portion of Vendée bordering the river
and the sea. I spent some weeks at a pretty little village
by the Atlantic. The costume of the peasants (les Mare-
chains) is very pretty. The women, with their hair
demurely parted in the middle, wear the most charming
white lace coiffes, black capes edged with fringe and
skirts pleated thickly round their waists. I thought for
a moment if they could but see a hobble skirt ! The men
wear black turned-up felt hats, with ribbons hanging
from the side, short black coats coming to the waist and
long black trousers. They have many interesting customs
and dances, one of the latter I saw performed on the sands,
and it was very pretty. Here by the sea is an enormous

The third division of Vendée—La Plaine—is remark-
able on first sight, by the absence of the dividing trees.
Here one, sees first an ordinary hedge or wire pailing as
in Ireland. I was motoring lately to a town of La Plaine,
which I know well : it is called Fontenay. On either
side of the highway were acres of vines laden with heavy
purple grapes, and only separated from the road by a
small wire pailing; not even barbed wire! Evidently
the passers-by are honest and differentiate between meum
and haon. In Fontenay there is, again, a large forest
belonging to Government, who sell the wood for fuel and
permit the well-to-do to hunt the sanglier (wild pig).
Deep in this forest years ago dwelt a hermit by name Pére
Montfort. Of course we visited the wood and climbed
down a precipitous path to reach the lonely grotto, hol-
lowed in a rock where he spent his days. It looks down
on a charming valley that slopes to the river. Somehow
I thought of St. Kevin, but here there was no Kathleen
to make trouble! The grotto itself presents the usual
features ‘hted candles, stalls at which are sold picture
postcards, images, etc. Pilgrims come here in great
numbers and miracles are attributed to the holy man.
On leaving the forest we arrived at the ruined Chateau de
Merveut, which was beseiged by King John Lackland in
1214. It stands upon a great height and commands a
most charming view of river and country. There is a
legend telling of one Mélusine who haunts the Castle and
wails and crys when death or evil is coming on the family
of Maulious.

Perhaps it was thinking of St. Kevin that made me find
the roads of Merveut so reminiscent of Glendalough.
Anyhow, I felt quite thrilled when my friend said : * I will
now show you the meeting of the waters !’ and there
actually below us I saw the rivers Mer and Veut (from
which the village takes its name) mingling their waters
in the most delightful valley I ever beheld. Tell it not in
Gath—but it beats our famous ‘ valley so sweet ” by
long odds. As alas ! I have not the pen of the celebrated
Thomas, I shall attempt no further description. The
town of Fontenay would rejoice the heart of the antiquar-
ian. It is full of relics of a bygone age. Some of the old
walls remain. I saw also a wonderful fountain pur
rénaissance built in the reign of Frangois I.

I must, however, conclude this already lengthy article
and reserve the curios of Fontenay for another occasion.

OR decades it was drummed into our ears that,

as a nation, we were the guardians of all the ills

to which flesh is heir. People talked, not with us, but

-ab us. And, indeed, persistency had its reward, for

many amongst us came to believe that we were really

a race of ““Incapables,”’ vastly inferior to the worst
England could show. And so forth.

But the history of the politico-national nfovement in
Ireland is an account of a people who were attacked
—nay, all but overcome—by Privilege, Influence, and
Wealth ; who faced those natural enemies; and who are
now engaged in overcoming them. In general it is a
picture of which we may feel proud.

English Liberalism, that is to say, the heart of Non-
conformity, ison the sameroad, but hasnot yet arrived at
our stage. Let. there be no mistake, Liberalism is not
engaged in passing aggressive laws, such as will put
the people on top. It is only enacting certain mea-
sures (e.g., the Parliament Aect, the Bill to abolish
plural voting), which will thereafter enable the parties
of the people to contest General Elections on level terms
with the Ascendancy party.

For me this fact is but a recent discovery. The
Ascendancy crowd adopt exactly the same tactics in
England as they do in Ireland. In spite of that metal-
lic, unattractive thing, known as the ‘° Nonconfor-
mist conscience,”’ one begins to feel sympathy for
English Nonconformity on account of the methods of
its opponents—methods so well known in Ireland.

COLLARING THE FLAG.

Liberalism, in the opinion of the plutocrats, is every
whit as ““ disloyal > as Irish Nationalism. To cite an
instance. The writer recently addressed a large meet-
ing in Kent—a Tory county. The dimensions of the
meeting were due to the advertising poster—a huge,
garish Union Jack, which caught the eye everywhere.
The Liberal agent explained that this kind of poster was
occasionally necessary in order to point out to the Tories
of Kent that the British National Flag was not their
special perquisite!

SHONEENISM.

Some years ago it was rarely considered respectable
to espouse the National Cause. So throughout exten-
sive districts of England to-day, Toryism is the badge
of superiority. Even in democratic areas there are not
wanting thousands of jellyfish, with all the character-
istics of Irish shoneenism. These vote Tory for no
other reason than that they still believe that the Tories
are the ‘‘ gentlemanly party.”’ Tor example: In a
Hampshire constituency hundreds of Liberals voted
Tory because the blood of the Liberal candidate was
not sufficiently blue to give him the entré to the local
““ society >’ of pensioned naval officers. That is Rath-
mines ab its best.

PARSON-RIDDEN ENGLAND.

T myself have heard parsons denouncing Ireland for
sheltering such hordes of tyrannising pricsts.  This,

« before us over Dolly’s Brae.””” This was the
refrain recently sung by the leaders of the students of
the Belfast University, who ratified the proposal of
their superiors in repudiating the coming Irish Parlia-
‘ment.  The Catholic students strove in vain to
stem the tide of bigotry and intolerance among the
students, just as the four well-meaning Catholic mem-
bers of the Senate strove to stem the same spirit in the
Senate. The latter body declared for Campbell’s pro-
posal for ““ safeguarding "’ this great ** Protestant Uni-
versity ” from the rapacity of the new Irish Parlia-
ment, although ‘‘ safeguards ’ ad nausecam have been
put in the Home Rule Bill for the ‘‘ Dolly’s Brae '’
men. The Senate have evinced the real ‘ Kick the
Pope " spirit also, although the sentiment is concealed
under long-winded resolutions and amendments. I
suppose we may soon expect the tunes, “ We’ll Kick
the Pope before us over Dolly’s Brae,” to be played at
the gates of St. Malachy’s College, and the Dominican
Convent, Falls Road—* Queen’s University Hostels.”’
Now, as many readers of the LEADER may not know the
meaning of ‘ Dolly’s Brae ’ as an Orange refrain, T’ll
give them a sketeh of it. 1t oceurred in the year 1849
—sixty-three years ago. Now if this phrase occurred
in the mouth of an ignorant Orangeman, it might be
somewhat excusable on the ground of ignorance, but
occurring in the song of a crowd of University Graduates
and Undergraduates, iv cannot be so excused, as they
know well what it means to Catholics, viz., a blood-
thirsty and murderous attack on the houses and persons
of poor, defenceless old men and women.

This brutal onslaught, as I said, took place in the year
1849, and I give its history in the language of a Protes-
tant historian, John Mitchel. Here it is:—‘ The Par-
liamentary Session of 1850 is further notable as the
occasion of a discussion upon the Orange outrage at
Dolly’s Brae, near Castlewellan in the County Down.
The transaction had taken place in the July of the
year before, at the usual celebration of the Orange
Anniversary. It happened in this manner:—The
Orangemen of various districts of that region had
assembled, marching by various routes, at the splendid
demesne called Tollymore Park, the seat of the BEarl
of Roden, one of the highest dignitaries of their Order.

A Dbright Christmas all lcok forward to, for brightness was
brought from Heaven by tho little Infant of Bethlehem.

Waalth can purchase much that is cheerful, and can diffuse its
rays to onlighten the gloom of sickness and want.

An Irish memorial of Irish gencrosity, raised by the Benevolent,
supported by tho Charitable Friends of the Poor, St. Michael's
Hospital affords to the feary sufferers the solace and relief which
they need moro at Christmas than at any other time, as they are
debarred from the little home festivitics which even the most
destitute can procure at that open-handed, open-pursed season.

The harbour, tho railway, the bicycls, tho tramway, the city,
as well as the surrounding districts, send patients, who, from
accident or ill-halth, receive in St. Michacl’s Hospital the medical
treatment, nursing, and nourishment they so much nced when
stricken down.

To keep the Wards, Dispensaries, Operation Theatre, etc.,
properly equipped; to store daily the quantity of Food and
Medicine necessary; to repair houtly the wear and tear of time;
to furnish coal, gas, etc—all this ontails an expenditure which
would appear exorbitant to one who had not practical experience
of outlay, but which, nevertheless, demands constantly well-
ciphered cheques to defray. The Sisters of Mercy, therefore, con-
fidently appeal to the proverbial liberality of the benefactors of
the suffering poor to give them such help as will enable them ta
continue this great work of mercy. They are deeply grateful to
all who havo perseveringly and cordially supported St. Michael’s
Hospital since its opening, and whose hundredfold rewards will be
bestowed here and hereafter by the Almighty Good God, Who
has never been outdone in generosity.
